cards.title: The Observability Gap in Data Systems
cards.teaser: Application engineers have logs, metrics, and traces. Data engineers often have "the dashboard looked wrong this morning." That gap is the observability problem.

runs.text: Software observability — logs, metrics, distributed traces — is a mature discipline. Data observability is newer and less standardized, which is why data failures tend to be discovered by consumers rather than producers.
The observability questions data engineers need to answer:

items.text: Did it complete successfully, and when?
Did it produce the expected data?
Correct row count, correct time range, correct quality.
Where did the data come from?
Which source tables fed into this model, and which jobs transformed them?
Which upstream job introduced the incorrect value?
Was it the data or the logic?

runs.text: The patterns in this chapter address each of these. Together, they form a
— the instrumentation that makes data systems debuggable.

"Shift-left" in data observability means catching anomalies in the pipeline, not in the dashboard. Every minute between when bad data enters the system and when someone notices it increases the cost of the fix.
Like adding metrics and traces to a backend service — without them, debugging is "I added print statements."

cards.title: Data Lineage
cards.teaser: Lineage tracks which source data fed into which downstream table. Without it, debugging data quality issues means manually tracing every upstream dependency.

runs.text: is the directed graph of how data flows from sources through transformations to destinations. A complete lineage graph lets you answer: "this dashboard metric is wrong — which upstream table is the source, and which job produced it?"
tracks not just which tables are dependencies, but which columns. "The
." Column-level lineage is more useful for debugging but harder to collect.
"this job reads tables A and B and writes table C." Coarser but easier to collect automatically.

traces data across system boundaries — from the operational database through the warehouse through the BI layer to the dashboard. End-to-end lineage is rare but extremely valuable when a dashboard is wrong.

items.text: macros build a lineage graph automatically as part of the project manifest.
OpenLineage (a standard protocol) instruments Airflow and Spark operators to emit lineage events to a compatible backend (Marquez, Atlan, DataHub).
data catalog tools (Collibra, Alation, DataHub) build and expose lineage graphs.

runs.text: dbt projects with consistent use of
get lineage almost for free. If you're hand-writing
Like a call stack in a debugger — you can see exactly where this value came from and which function set it.

cards.title: Pipeline Metrics
cards.teaser: Instrument every pipeline run with duration, row counts, error counts, and retry counts. These metrics make capacity planning, debugging, and SLA management possible.

runs.text: are quantitative measurements about how a pipeline ran, emitted consistently so they can be aggregated, trended, and alerted on.

items.text: how long each task and DAG run took. Trend line over time detects gradual degradation before it becomes an incident.
how many rows were read from source, how many passed validation, how many were written to destination. A 0-row load is detectable before it corrupts downstream.
how many records were quarantined or failed validation.
how many times did a task retry before succeeding or failing. High retry rates indicate flaky sources or infrastructure.
for streaming pipelines, how far behind the consumer is from the producer.
Airflow stores task duration and state in its metadata database. Query it.
emit metrics to Prometheus, Datadog, CloudWatch from within pipeline code.
Great Expectations and Soda emit run metadata as structured results that can be stored and queried.

explanation.code:
run_start = time.time()
row_count = write_to_destination(df)
duration = time.time() - run_start
emit_metric("pipeline.rows_written", row_count, tags=["pipeline:orders_daily"])
emit_metric("pipeline.duration_seconds", duration, tags=["pipeline:orders_daily"])

runs.text: Duration trending is one of the highest-value pipeline metrics. A job that ran in 10 minutes six months ago and now runs in 90 minutes has a problem — and the data is already there to see it.
Like application performance monitoring for code — you need the numbers to know which part is slow before you can improve it.

cards.title: Freshness SLAs
cards.teaser: Define how stale data is allowed to be for each table, then measure and alert on violations. Freshness is a first-class service level agreement, not an assumption.

runs.text: (service level agreement) defines the maximum acceptable staleness for a given table. "The
table must have data no older than 2 hours by 9 AM each business day" is a freshness SLA.

items.text: Consumers don't know a pipeline is stuck unless they check timestamps. A dashboard that says "yesterday's revenue" but shows numbers from three days ago is more dangerous than an error message.
On-call engineers need objective criteria for whether a pipeline is within acceptable range or an incident.
Data contracts between teams need freshness specifications, not just schema specifications.

explanation.code:
-- dbt freshness check
version: 2
sources:
  - name: stripe
    tables:
      - name: charges
        freshness:
          warn_after: {count: 1, period: hour}
          error_after: {count: 3, period: hour}
        loaded_at_field: created_at

items.text: a table that normally receives 100 rows per hour but receives zero for 3 hours might be within its freshness SLA but still indicate a problem.
tables with lower weekend volume will appear "stale" on Monday morning even if they're correct. Freshness thresholds should be schedule-aware.
freshness checks only detect that the latest row timestamp is old. They don't detect whether yesterday's data is complete (volume check) or correct (quality check).

runs.text: Build freshness checks into the pipeline's self-check step and into a consumer-facing data catalog. Consumers should be able to see freshness status without paging the data team.
Like a "last updated" timestamp on a shared document — without it, you don't know whether to trust the numbers or refresh the page.

cards.title: On-Call Ergonomics
cards.teaser: A pipeline that works great in normal conditions but produces incomprehensible errors at 2am is a liability. Design for the worst debugging context, not the best.

runs.text: is the practice of designing pipelines so that the on-call engineer who receives an alert at 2am can diagnose and resolve the issue without deep context of how the pipeline was originally built.

The failure mode to avoid: an on-call engineer who didn't build the pipeline receives an alert, opens the DAG, and finds a wall of custom Python, opaque task names, no comments about what the pipeline is supposed to do, and error messages that say
When the circuit breaker trips, emit a message that says why: "Expected ≥10,000 rows. Got 0. Source: Stripe charges API. Possible cause: API outage or credential expiry."

A short document linked from the DAG or alert that describes: what this pipeline does, what the common failure modes are, and the first three steps to investigate. 90% of incidents are one of three causes; a runbook that covers the top 3 saves hours.
A DAG where task failure cascades through 20 dependent tasks is harder to diagnose than one where failures are isolated. Limit fan-out in critical paths.
Once the root cause is fixed, the on-call engineer should be able to clear the stuck tasks and re-run without risk of data corruption.
After every incident, add a runbook entry for the failure mode that caused it. The fifth occurrence of the same failure should take under 5 minutes to resolve.
Like leaving a clear note when you pass code to a colleague — the context you carry in your head is lost when they're debugging at 2am.

cards.title: Data Quality Dashboards
cards.teaser: Centralize quality metrics into a single view so the team and its consumers can see the health of the data layer at a glance.

runs.text: aggregates the results of expectation tests, freshness checks, anomaly alerts, and quarantine volumes into a single view — the "health dashboard" for the data layer.

items.text: last updated timestamp and freshness SLA status (green/yellow/red) for each critical table.
pass/fail per expectation test, trend over the last 30 days.
rows quarantined per source per day.
last successful run per DAG, success rate over the last 7 days.
active data quality issues with owning team and estimated resolution.
see at a glance whether anything is broken before investigating individual pipelines.
check whether the tables they're about to query have fresh, quality-checked data.
understand the health of the data layer without pinging an engineer.
Most orchestrators (Airflow, Prefect, Dagster) expose a UI with run history — use it as the source for pipeline status.
Great Expectations / Soda produce structured results that can be stored in a database and visualized with any BI tool.
Data catalog tools (DataHub, Atlan, Monte Carlo) offer built-in quality dashboards with cross-source aggregation.

runs.text: A quality dashboard that shows only "pass" and "fail" is less useful than one that shows trend lines. A table that passes today but has been trending toward failure for a week is more actionable than a table that failed today with no history.
Like a CI/CD dashboard for code — test pass rates and build health in one place, so you see the trend, not just today's state.

cards.title: Structured Logging for Pipelines
cards.teaser: Text logs tell you something happened. Structured logs tell you what happened, to which pipeline, in which run, on which date — queryable, aggregatable, actionable.

runs.text: emits log events as structured data (JSON or key-value pairs) rather than freeform text. A structured log is queryable; a text log requires regex to extract meaning.
The structured version is searchable by pipeline, aggregatable by rows_written, joinable to alert history by run_id.
Key fields to include in every pipeline log event:

items.text: — correlates all log events for one run.

runs.text: Log destinations: CloudWatch Logs, Datadog, Splunk, OpenSearch. Most support structured log ingestion and querying directly.
to every log event and link it in your alerting. "Find all logs for run 2026-05-01T06:00:00Z" turns a debugging session from 30 minutes to 2 minutes.
Like typed structs vs printf debugging — structured data is first-class; you can query it, aggregate it, and alert on it without writing a parser.

cards.title: Alerting Strategies
cards.teaser: An alert that fires every day for a non-urgent issue gets ignored. Alert fatigue is as dangerous as no alerts. Design alerts to be specific, routed correctly, and actionable.

runs.text: — the condition where on-call engineers begin ignoring alerts because too many fire for non-critical events — is a data observability failure mode as common as missing alerts.
data is actively wrong and consumers are affected. Example: the
table freshness is 8 hours past SLA on a business day. Routes to: on-call engineer's phone.
a quality check is failing but data is still loading. Example: the null rate in
spiked from 2% to 18%. Routes to: team Slack channel.
trend that needs investigation but isn't urgent. Example: pipeline duration has increased 40% over the past week. Routes to: weekly report or email.

items.text: What the expected vs actual value is.
A link to the runbook or dashboard.
A link to the failing pipeline run.

runs.text: An alert that says "data quality check failed" with no context is worse than no alert — it requires manual investigation to determine severity.

items.text: don't alert during expected maintenance windows or known data latencies.
if 5 tables all fail for the same reason (upstream source outage), fire one alert, not five.
wait 15 minutes before paging; many transient failures self-resolve.

runs.text: Every alert that fires without being actioned trains the team to ignore alerts. Audit your alert history monthly: which alerts resolved without action? Those are candidates for demotion or suppression.
Like a good code review comment — specific, actionable, with enough context to understand the issue without looking everything up from scratch.

cards.title: Column-Level vs Table-Level Lineage
cards.teaser: Table-level lineage tells you which tables feed which tables. Column-level lineage tells you which specific column in the source produced which column in the output — the difference matters for debugging.

runs.text: traces the relationships between tables: "table C was produced by a job that read tables A and B." It answers "which upstream sources could have caused this table to be wrong?"
." It answers "which specific source field, transformation, and filter produced this specific column?"

When table-level lineage is enough: you need to understand data flow for governance (which tables contain PII that flows downstream), dependency management (which pipelines to re-run after a source change), or impact analysis (what breaks if this table is deleted).

When column-level lineage is needed: a specific metric is wrong and you need to trace the computation back to the source field. A regulatory request requires proving which source data contributed to a reported figure. A schema change in a specific source column needs to be tracked forward through all derived columns.
Column-level lineage is harder to collect:

items.text: dbt provides column-level lineage through its graph if models are written with explicit column selection (not
OpenLineage captures column-level lineage from Spark operations automatically.
Data catalog tools (DataHub, Atlan) display column-level lineage when available.

runs.text: Cost of column-level lineage: collecting it requires parsing SQL and tracing derivations, which is compute-intensive and imperfect for complex transformations. Not every pipeline or tool can produce it.
Build table-level lineage first — it's achievable with all orchestrators and dbt. Add column-level incrementally for the most critical tables where debugging granularity justifies the investment.
Like stack traces vs thread dumps — both are lineage; one tells you which function called which; the other tells you what value each variable held.

cards.title: SLA Breach Response Patterns
cards.teaser: When a freshness or quality SLA is breached, the response sequence matters as much as the technical fix. A defined playbook resolves breaches faster than improvised investigation.

runs.text: An SLA breach is not the same as a pipeline failure. A breach means a consumer-facing guarantee has been violated — it has business impact. The response must be proportional.
is the breach real or a monitoring false positive? Check whether the table actually has stale data, not just that a freshness check fired.
who depends on this table? Is a dashboard currently visible to stakeholders showing wrong or old data? Is a model inference job blocked waiting for fresh features? Impact determines urgency.

notify affected consumers before they notice and ping you. "The orders table is 4 hours behind SLA; we're investigating; estimated resolution by 9 AM." A proactive message is 10× better than a reactive one.
follow the standard diagnostic order — networking → authentication → source health → data quality → downstream. The runbook should cover the three most common causes for this specific pipeline.
fix the root cause, re-run the pipeline, verify the table freshness and quality before declaring resolution.
document the timeline and root cause. Update the runbook with the new failure mode. If the same breach has happened before, escalate to a permanent fix.
Write a status page entry for the breach, even if it's just an internal Slack thread. "Known issue, being investigated, ETA 9 AM" stops the stream of "is the data broken?" messages.
Like incident response in SRE — the playbook exists so you don't improvise under pressure; the steps are the same every time; the specifics change.
